skill.name: pydeseq2
skill.description: Guide PyDESeq2 differential expression analysis for bulk RNA-seq count data, including design setup, Wald tests, FDR correction, result export, and plots.
skill.summary: Run and interpret PyDESeq2 workflows for bulk RNA-seq differential expression analysis.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution and weak cryptography patterns, but review found these are mostly Markdown/code-token false positives around PyDESeq2 documentation. The main real concern is documented pickle loading, which is safe only for trusted local artifacts; the script also writes expected local analysis outputs.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Shows Loading Pickle Files
medium_findings.description: The workflow guide demonstrates pickle.load for saved count, metadata, and AnnData objects. Loading pickle data from untrusted sources can execute code, so these examples should be treated as trusted-local-artifact patterns only.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly call pickle.load. The risk depends on file trust, but Python pickle loading is a well-known code execution hazard for untrusted inputs.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick findings are Markdown code fences and inline code references for Python examples and reference loading. I found no subprocess, os.system, exec, eval, curl, or wget pattern in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is Markdown documentation and Python sample code, not Ruby or shell execution. Targeted search found no command execution APIs in the skill files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are DESeq2 Name False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography alerts appear to be triggered by the term DESeq2 and related documentation text. No cryptographic API, hash routine, cipher configuration, or password-handling code was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is PyDESeq2 documentation and script metadata. There is no cryptographic operation in the reviewed implementation.

low_findings.title: Local Result Files Are Written to User-Selected Output Directory
low_findings.description: The bundled script creates an output directory and writes CSV, pickle, and optional plot files. This is expected for an analysis script, but users should avoid pointing output at sensitive paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly writes analysis artifacts using a user-provided output directory. This is legitimate behavior, with low risk from accidental overwrite or unwanted local files.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The detected URLs point to official PyDESeq2 documentation and the upstream GitHub repository. I found no code that opens these URLs or transmits local data to a network endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are reference links in Markdown. There are no network client imports or request calls in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Finding Is Requirement Text
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance alert maps to documentation about Python and package requirements. No host probing, environment enumeration, or credential discovery behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section lists software requirements and optional visualization packages. It does not execute commands or inspect the host system.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documentation Shows Loading Pickle Files
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow guide demonstrates pickle.load for saved count, metadata, and AnnData objects. Loading pickle data from untrusted sources can execute code, so these examples should be treated as trusted-local-artifact patterns only.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly call pickle.load. The risk depends on file trust, but Python pickle loading is a well-known code execution hazard for untrusted inputs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem Output Operations
dangerous_patterns.description: The analysis script creates directories and writes result artifacts under the selected output path. This supports the advertised workflow but should be disclosed as local filesystem access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are explicit and necessary for the analysis workflow. They do not show malicious behavior or unauthorized path discovery.

content.value_statement: Bulk RNA-seq analysis requires careful count filtering, model design, and FDR interpretation. This skill guides PyDESeq2 workflows from data preparation to plots and result review.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to prepare count matrices and sample metadata for PyDESeq2.
Guides single-factor and multi-factor DESeq2 design formulas.
Shows how to run Wald tests, contrasts, FDR correction, and LFC shrinkage.
Provides a Python script for local CSV input, result export, and optional plots.
Helps interpret significant genes, volcano plots, MA plots, and adjusted p-values.
limitations: Requires valid bulk RNA-seq count data and matching sample metadata.
Does not replace statistical review by a qualified bioinformatician.
The bundled script runs locally and writes output files to disk.
Pickle examples should only be used with trusted local files.

use_cases.title: Compare Treated and Control Samples
use_cases.description: Run a standard two-group differential expression workflow and identify genes with adjusted p-values below a chosen threshold.

use_cases.title: Account for Batch Effects
use_cases.description: Build a multi-factor design that adjusts for batch or covariates while testing the biological condition of interest.

use_cases.title: Port DESeq2 Workflows to Python
use_cases.description: Translate familiar DESeq2 analysis steps into PyDESeq2, pandas, and AnnData-based Python workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Check My Inputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my bulk RNA-seq count table and metadata layout for PyDESeq2. Tell me if rows, columns, sample IDs, or count values need changes before I run the analysis.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Basic Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me run PyDESeq2 for treated versus control samples. Use a condition design, filter low-count genes, run the contrast, and explain the key result columns.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Batch-Aware Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PyDESeq2 analysis plan for samples with batch and condition metadata. Explain the design formula order, contrast choice, and checks for confounding.

prompt_templates.title: Audit and Interpret Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my PyDESeq2 output summary. Explain significant genes, possible outliers, LFC shrinkage effects, volcano and MA plot patterns, and follow-up quality checks.

output_examples.input: User asks how to compare treated and control bulk RNA-seq samples.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step PyDESeq2 workflow covering count orientation, metadata alignment, low-count filtering, design setup, contrast selection, and adjusted p-value interpretation.

output_examples.input: User asks whether batch should be included in the design.
output_examples.output: A model recommendation that places batch before condition, explains why adjustment matters, and lists checks for sample balance and confounding.

output_examples.input: User asks what the significant gene table means.
output_examples.output: A plain-language interpretation of base mean, log2 fold change, raw p-value, adjusted p-value, and practical ranking criteria.

best_practices: Verify that count matrix sample IDs exactly match metadata sample IDs before fitting.
Filter low-count genes before testing to improve power and reduce noisy results.
Use adjusted p-values for significance decisions and document the chosen threshold.
anti_patterns: Do not use normalized expression values as input counts for PyDESeq2.
Do not ignore batch or covariates when they are part of the experiment.
Do not load pickle files from untrusted sources.

faq.question: What data does this skill expect?
faq.answer: It expects bulk RNA-seq integer counts and sample metadata with matching sample identifiers.

faq.question: Can it handle single-cell RNA-seq?
faq.answer: It is focused on bulk RNA-seq. Single-cell workflows need different assumptions and preprocessing.

faq.question: Does it run the analysis automatically?
faq.answer: It provides guidance and a local Python script. Users control the files, parameters, and execution environment.

faq.question: What plots can it help create?
faq.answer: It covers volcano plots and MA plots, with optional matplotlib output in the bundled script.

faq.question: How should significant genes be selected?
faq.answer: Use adjusted p-values, often padj below 0.05, and consider log2 fold change and biological context.

faq.question: Are pickle files safe to use?
faq.answer: Only load pickle files that you created or fully trust. Untrusted pickle files can execute code when loaded.
